title: Welcome to Corinth
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Welcome to Corinth_ 2 Corinthians 1_1-2 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Introduction: Beginning the series in 2 Corinthians
Background: the church at Corinth and its history
Paul's greeting: apostle by the will of God
Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ
The occasion and purpose of 2 Corinthians
Overview of themes in the letter
Conclusion: Welcome to Corinth — a letter for struggling churches
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro introduces the series in 2 Corinthians by preaching from the opening greeting (1:1-2). He provides background on the church at Corinth, Paul's apostolic authority, and the occasion for the letter — setting the stage for an epistle that speaks directly to struggling churches and embattled ministries.
transcript:
## Introduction: Not Just Ancient Correspondence

When we open the Scriptures and read a letter like this one, we are not simply reading ancient correspondence. This is a real letter sent to a real group of people by a real man named Paul — but when we open it and read, we are not simply reading an ancient form of literature. We are hearing the living Christ address his church.

The Corinthian church was a real church, but it also represents a church — representing all of us. So when we read 2 Corinthians, you are reading Christ's word to us even today here at our church. This is especially true in a letter like 2 Corinthians, where theology is forged in suffering and pastoral ministry unfolds in weakness.

2 Corinthians is among the most personal letters in the New Testament. In it we meet an apostle who has been opposed, misunderstood, slandered, and deeply wounded by people whom he loved. Yet he remains utterly committed to Christ and to Christ's church. This letter teaches us what faithful ministry looks like when obedience is costly and appearances are deceiving.

## The Theme and Structure of the Series

We are starting today a series on 2 Corinthians. I had thought about a series on Romans, but the wisdom of the pastoral tradition is that Romans should be your last series in ministry — you need a lifelong ministry before you are able to preach faithfully through the book of Romans. So perhaps the next series will be on Romans.

As we begin this 26-part series through this letter, Paul directs us first not to controversy, not to suffering, not to rebuke — though there is plenty of all those things in this letter — but first to **identity, authority, and blessings**. So for today we are only going to look at the first two verses. In just these two verses Paul establishes the foundation for everything that follows in the letter.

This series is structured around the theme: **power, perfected in weakness** — because that is the theme of 2 Corinthians. The goal is not merely to study Paul's biography (though there is a great deal of that in this letter) or to resolve historical questions, but to shepherd us into a cross-shaped understanding of Christian living, Christian ministry, and suffering and hope under the new covenant.

If you read through 2 Corinthians, you see that it challenges and confronts nearly every instinct we have as modern Christians. We value strength, visibility, efficiency, charisma, success. Paul here says that Christ works most clearly through weakness, suffering, sincerity, and dependence — not the things we as modern people would naturally rely on.

Over the coming months this letter will recalibrate how we think about spiritual authority, faithful ministry, affliction-sharing comfort, repentance and restoration, generosity and stewardship, and perseverance and assurance. This series will move carefully from apostolic authority (verses 1–2 of chapter 1) through comfort and suffering, new covenant ministry, gospel generosity, spiritual warfare, and finally to self-examination in chapter 13. The goal is that we would be shaped not by isolated texts, but by the whole message of the Scriptures.

Today's sermon is intentionally foundational for everything that follows. Paul does not begin with correction, controversy, or comfort. He begins with **calling, covenant identity, and blessings**. Before we can understand the comfort and affliction of verses 3–11, the power and weakness of chapter 12, or the call to examine ourselves in chapter 13, we must first understand who speaks, to whom he speaks, and on what basis God deals with his church.

## Apostolic Authority: Paul, Apostle by the Will of God (v. 1a)

> *"Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, and Timothy our brother."* (2 Corinthians 1:1)

This letter begins like any other first-century letter sent by one who had authority over his audience and expected them to follow what he wrote. It is not just a friendly introduction. Paul believed that the church should listen to what he had to say, that his words were binding upon the church.

But notice how he does it — **humbly and theologically**. He does not say, "Paul, an apostle, who planted many churches" or "Paul, an apostle, who has had great success." He says: "*an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God.*"

His authority is not self-generated. It does not rest on charisma, success, or public approval. His apostleship rests on God's eternal decree. This aligns directly with WCF chapter 3, paragraph 1, which teaches that God ordains all things freely and unchangeably. Paul's calling, his conversion, his sufferings, and even his conflicts with Corinth — which are many — were not accidents. God's sovereign purpose governed them all.

Paul's authority is ministerial, not lordly. He is not there to lord over people, but to minister to them as a servant of Jesus Christ. He speaks with authority because he speaks Christ's word, not his own. What we are considering here is Christ's word to his church.

## Shared Ministry: And Timothy Our Brother (v. 1b)

Notice that Paul does not stand alone. He says, "Paul, an apostle... and **Timothy our brother**."

This is not mere formality. Timothy was Paul's true son in the faith, converted under Paul's ministry and shaped through years of mentorship. Timothy had been sent to Corinth before. He knew the church, its struggles, and its resistance to Paul's teachings. And though Paul held apostolic office, he calls Timothy "our brother" — reminding us that leadership in Christ's church is relational, humble, and shared.

There is no king other than Jesus in the Church of Jesus Christ. There is no single apostle today. Years later, Paul writes to Timothy and says, "Don't let anyone despise your youth" — meaning Timothy was even younger when Paul wrote to Corinth. Timothy's youth, Timothy's weakness, anticipate the central theme of this letter: **God displays his power through weak servants**.

God is not after our strength; he is after our weaknesses. It is through those weaknesses that we are made strong to serve him.

Paul and Timothy's relationship shows us at least three things:

### 1. Spiritual Fatherhood
Paul did not just plant churches. He invested deeply in people, particularly in men. Timothy represents the fruit of long-term discipleship. How we need this — men investing in men in long-term discipleship so that together we may be shaped into the image of Jesus Christ.

### 2. Shared Ministry
There is no lone ranger in the Church of Jesus Christ. Ministry is shared. Timothy had been sent to Corinth before. He knew the people, their struggles, their resistance. Including his name reinforces continuity and pastoral care: *We all care for you. You are not alone.*

### 3. Ministerial Humility
By all measures Paul outranks Timothy — yet he calls him "our brother." Authority in Christ's church never negates fraternity. We are brothers and sisters. This reflects a deeply Reformed and Presbyterian principle: Christ governs his church through connected, accountable, relational ministry — not through isolated authority figures. Christ has given elders to rule over his church, not a single tyrant to lord it over.

## The Church of God in Corinth (v. 1c)

> *"...to the church of God which is at Corinth, with all the saints who are in all Achaia."* (2 Corinthians 1:1)

Corinth was one of the most important cities in the Roman Empire — a population of about 80,000, situated on a narrow isthmus that controlled major trade routes. Ships from one harbor were sometimes carried overland to the other harbor. It was a wealthy, cosmopolitan, influential city, but also morally notorious, marked by sexual immorality and religious pluralism. The word "Corinthian" had become a byword for debauchery. In many ways Corinth resembles the modern Western city: prosperous, ambitious, sophisticated, and deeply broken.

Yet Paul addresses the Corinthians gathered there as **"the church of God."** He does not say "to you Corinthians" — he says "to the church of God in Corinth." This is covenantal language. The church does not belong to Paul. It does not belong to Corinth. It belongs to God. WCF chapter 25 teaches that the visible church is God's appointed means of gathering and perfecting the saints, even when deeply imperfect. The church's identity rests not on her faithfulness but on God's promise.

### Part of Something Larger: The Saints Throughout Achaia

By addressing the saints throughout Achaia — the Roman province encompassing southern Greece, including Athens, Cenchreae (home of Phoebe, the woman who carried the letter of Romans), and the surrounding regions — Paul reminds the Corinthians that they are part of a **regional network of churches**, not an isolated spiritual outpost.

This matters because Corinth tended toward spiritual elitism and local pride. Paul quietly but firmly reorients them: *you belong to something far bigger than yourselves.*

There is a movement today among some younger Christians that says only the city matters — only urban ministry matters. But what about people in rural areas? Are little churches not worthy of pastors? Consider Spurgeon. He was converted as a fifteen-year-old in a small chapel in the English countryside. A storm prevented him from reaching the church he intended to attend. He ducked into a tiny Primitive Methodist chapel with perhaps fifteen people present. The pastor had not even come because of the weather. An older layman stood up and read from Isaiah 45: "Look to Me, and be saved, all you ends of the earth." He was a very plain man, and he stuck closely to the text. He looked at Spurgeon — because in a church of fifteen, you know who the visitors are — and said, "Young man, you look troubled. Look to your Savior even now." And it was in that little rural chapel that one of the greatest preachers who ever lived was converted.

Not only the city matters. Everywhere matters. Christ has one church across many places.

WCF chapter 25 teaches that the visible church consists of all who confess the true religion together with their children, spread across the world, yet united under Christ as their one head. We are part of something much bigger than these walls. Spurgeon observed that the Lord's Day is like a tidal wave — looking at it across 24 hours and every time zone, there are people everywhere worshiping the Lord, and that wave sweeps over the entire world not with destruction but with the praises of the Savior, Jesus Christ.

By addressing the saints throughout Achaia, Paul also underscores that **local churches are accountable beyond themselves**. No congregation exists independently. We are united to other churches. Believers share a common faith, a common baptism, and a common hope. That is why when someone joins our church having been baptized elsewhere, we do not say "it wasn't our water, so it doesn't count." We are united by one baptism.

### You Are Saints

Notice what Paul calls the Christians in Corinth — and us: **saints**. You might find it strange that people who don't look like saints and don't always behave like saints are called saints. But being a saint has nothing to do with who you are; it has everything to do with what Christ has done for you. You have been set apart to be his people. Sainthood flows from union with Christ, not from moral perfection. They are saints because God has set them apart in Christ, even while they remain pilgrims and strangers in a fallen world.

Brothers and sisters, we are saints — **by calling, not by achievement**. Do not start polishing your halo just yet.

The church has always been scattered yet united. Always small in the world's eyes, yet precious to God. Always weak in itself, yet sustained by grace. The citizens of Ephesus dragged the Christians before the authorities with this accusation: "These are weak servants, peasants, the lowest of society — and these are the people who are turning the world upside down." Can that be said of us today? Are we, by our faithfulness to Christ and the proclamation of his gospel, turning the world upside down? Or are we more concerned about keeping the current order and not upsetting anyone?

The saints in Corinth were not alone. Neither are we today.

## Grace and Peace from God (v. 2)

> *"Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ."* (2 Corinthians 1:2)

Grace always precedes peace. **Grace** is God's unmerited favor toward sinners in Christ. **Peace** is the result of finding that grace. Peace is reconciliation with God — and Paul's greeting here reflects the doctrine that God is responsible for salvation from beginning to end, because it is by his grace.

Peace is not mere emotional calm. It is covenantal reconciliation through justification by faith alone, by grace alone:

> *"Therefore, having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom also we have access by faith into this grace in which we stand, and rejoice in hope of the glory of God."* (Romans 5:1–2)

This is the peace we are all looking for. And it can only be found in the grace of God through Jesus Christ. Not how much you read your Bible, not what sins you've committed, not how much you give, not how often you attend church — nothing that starts with "I" — but the grace of God in Jesus Christ. That is where peace comes from.

Notice also that this blessing is explicitly **Trinitarian**: grace and peace from God the Father and the Son, applied by the Spirit. The Trinity is all over the Bible. If the Spirit has opened your eyes, you cannot read it without seeing the triune existence of God.

And notice that Paul begins this letter — a letter that is going to contain a great deal of correction — with blessing. Because **obedience flows from grace, not from fear**.

## Six Applications

If these truths shaped Paul's relationship with Corinth, they must shape how we think, live, and serve as Christ's church today.

**1. Submit joyfully to Christ's appointed Word.** Paul expected the Corinthians to follow what he said — not because it was Paul's word, but because it was Christ's word. By implication, that applies to us as well.

**2. Value ministry in difficult places.** God delighted to plant his church in Corinth — one of the most morally corrupt cities in the empire. He still delights to plant churches in hard places. One of the reasons our state is in the condition it is, is that Christians have fled. Do not do that.

**3. Embrace shared, relational leadership.** God has appointed elders to rule over his church — not a single authority figure. Embrace your elders as gifts from Christ's hand. Paul and Timothy model patient, humble, long-term shared ministry. I genuinely do not understand churches in which elders do not talk to one another between meetings.

**4. Love the church despite her flaws.** Paul loved the church of God in Corinth — and if you read 1 Corinthians, you know what that church was like. People today say, "I want to be a New Testament church." I don't. The New Testament exists because those churches were really messed up and God was writing to them saying, "Stop being so messed up." But Paul loved the church despite her flaws. If Christ does not abandon his church, neither should you.

**5. Rest in your identity as saints.** Brothers and sisters, you are not defined by your failures, but by God's calling in Christ. You are not your sin, if you are a follower of Jesus Christ. You are a saint of God.

**6. Live from grace, not for grace.** Do not live thinking, "If I do these things, I will get more grace from God" — as if anything you do could merit his favor. Nothing merits grace, because grace by definition is unmerited. Live *from* the grace that God has already given you. Every command in this letter flows from grace already given. God has already given you the grace to follow him. Christ already died on the cross and rose again. You do not need to wait for more grace — you do it now.

## Conclusion

2 Corinthians opens not with triumph, but with grace. Not with strength, but with calling. Not with perfection, but with covenant faithfulness. As we journey through this letter, may we learn to see Christ's power perfected in weakness. May we see his grace sufficient for every trial, and his peace guarding his church — in Corinth and throughout the world, including here as we follow him.
